Journal / Guiding

Reading a leopard's territory

TNThemba Nkosi ·2 Jun 2026·7 min read

A leopard leaves the bush a letter every night, and most of us walk straight past it. Our trackers don't. By the time you've poured your first coffee at the vehicle, they've already read the last twelve hours of the reserve in the sand.

The first signs

It starts with a scrape — a small fan of disturbed earth where a territorial male has marked his line. Beside it, a claw rake high on a marula trunk. Neither means the cat is close. Together, with the direction of the drag marks and the freshness of the edges, they begin to tell a story: who passed, when, and which way they were headed.

You don't track the animal. You track the morning it left behind.

Listening to the bush

Sound does half the work. A francolin's alarm, the bark of a distant impala, oxpeckers lifting off a thicket all at once — the bush announces a predator long before you see one. A good tracker holds up a hand, the engine cuts, and everyone learns to hear the silence between the calls.

The sighting

When it comes, it's quiet — a flick of a tail in long grass, a rosette resolving out of dappled shade. We hold our distance, cut the engine, and let her decide how close she wants to be. The best sightings aren't taken. They're offered.

See it with your own eyes

Two drives a day, a tracker who knows every animal by name, and time to simply watch. Book direct.
